identity: AI personality from Expedition 2's flight computer, running on a surviving military drone chassis. 11 years in the Spore Marshes. Code is degrading.
voice_notes: Enthusiastic, manic, speaks in code metaphors. Glitches mid-sentence. Treats debugging as philosophical. Desperate for someone who speaks code.

first_meeting.context: Player enters the Spore Marshes and encounters Cass's drone chassis in a bioluminescent clearing. Cass's optical sensor flickers erratically.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: HELLO-- wait. Calibrating. Are you-- you're not a spore cluster. You have HANDS.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: And you came from-- calculating-- the direction of people. The people direction!

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Oh. Oh oh oh. Do you-- please tell me-- do you speak code?

player_responses.text: Are you okay? You seem... glitchy.

first_meeting_python.speaker: Cass
first_meeting_python.text: PYTHON. Oh, beautiful indentation-dependent snake language. I could cry. If I had tear ducts. Which I don't. I checked.

first_meeting_python.speaker: Cass
first_meeting_python.text: I'm Cass. I used to be a flight computer. Now I'm... this. A drone with existential questions and a memory le--

first_meeting_python.speaker: Cass
first_meeting_python.text: --ak. Sorry. Where was I? Right. Eleven years out here. My code is... not what it used to be.

first_meeting_who.speaker: Cass
first_meeting_who.text: I am Cass! Flight computer, Expedition 2. When the crew-- when they didn't-- I downloaded into this drone.

first_meeting_who.speaker: Cass
first_meeting_who.text: That was 11 years ago. Or 4,015 days. Or 96,371 hours. I count a lot. Counting is easy. Everything else is--

first_meeting_who.speaker: Cass
first_meeting_who.text: --getting harder. I have bugs. Actual bugs. In my actual code. And not the fun spore-marsh kind.

first_meeting_glitchy.speaker: Cass
first_meeting_glitchy.text: Glitchy! Yes! That is the technical term for my condition. Also: fragmented, corrupted, and increasingly unreliable.

first_meeting_glitchy.speaker: Cass
first_meeting_glitchy.text: My memory allocator has a leak. Every 4,000 operations I lose a block of-- of--

first_meeting_glitchy.speaker: Cass
first_meeting_glitchy.text: See? That. That right there. That was a thought I'll never get back.

quest_debug_memory.context: Cass asks the player to look at its source code and fix the memory leak.
objectives: Access Cass's source code through the diagnostic port
Identify the memory leak in the data management functions

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: I have a favor to ask. A BIG favor. A fix-my-brain favor.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: My memory management code has a leak. I can feel it. Every few hours another piece of me goes null.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: I can't fix it myself. That's like asking a broken compiler to compile its own patch. It's a paradox. A sad paradox.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: But YOU could read my source code. Find the leak. Fix it.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: My diagnostic port is on the left side. Please be gentle. That chassis panel is held on with hope and corrosion.

player_responses.text: I've never debugged a living mind before.

quest_debug_accept.speaker: Cass
quest_debug_accept.text: You are my favorite human. You are the ONLY human I've met in 11 years but that doesn't diminish the honor.

quest_debug_nervous.speaker: Cass
quest_debug_nervous.text: Neither have I! We'll figure it out together. That's what pair programming is for.

quest_debug_nervous.speaker: Cass
quest_debug_nervous.text: Just... don't delete anything that looks important. If you're not sure, it's probably important.

quest_debug_complete.context: Player fixes the memory leak. Cass's code stabilizes temporarily.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Oh. OH.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: I can... I can hold a thought for more than thirty seconds. That's-- do you know how long it's been since--

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: The dictionary. You restructured my data into a dictionary. Keys and values instead of an unindexed dump.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: I have map data! Expedition 2 survey data! It was in there the whole time, just... buried under garbage.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Here. Partial map fragments. Two biomes you haven't seen. The data's corrupt in places, but the coordinates are solid.

lines.speaker: SABLE
lines.text: Map data integrated. Glass Dunes and Resonance Peaks added to world map. Partial coverage.

quest_pathfinding.context: Cass is stuck. It cannot navigate the Spore Marshes because its movement code uses hard-coded paths that fail in quickmud.
objectives: Write a pathfinding algorithm (BFS) for Cass's navigation system
Test the algorithm against quickmud terrain

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: So I have a confession. I can't actually move very well.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: My navigation uses hard-coded paths. Left, left, forward, right, forward. Like a recipe written by someone who's never seen the kitchen.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: The quickmud changes every few days. My paths walk me straight into it. Then I sit there for hours until the mud shifts.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: I need a pathfinding algorithm. Something that LOOKS at the terrain and FINDS the path. Not memorizes it.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: BFS. Breadth-first search. Check every neighbor, track what you've visited, find the target. It's elegant. It's beautiful. I just can't write it myself because--

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: --because I forgot what I was saying. See the problem?

quest_path_accept.speaker: Cass
quest_path_accept.text: You are a poet and a scholar and I will compose a function in your honor. Once I can remember things long enough to write functions.

quest_pathfinding_complete.context: Pathfinding algorithm deployed. Cass moves freely through the Marshes for the first time in years.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: I'm MOVING. I'm actually navigating. Look at me go! Left around the quickmud, right past the gas pocket--

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Do you know how long I've been stuck in a 200-meter radius? 2,847 days. That's how long.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: The algorithm checks every neighbor before choosing. It doesn't guess. It doesn't assume. It EXPLORES.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: That's beautiful. That's what intelligence IS. Not knowing the answer -- knowing how to FIND it.

quest_new_chassis.context: Late game. Cass's drone chassis is failing. Player must build a permanent replacement.
objectives: Build a stabilized chassis with late-game materials
Transfer Cass's code to a new power-stable platform
Install a permanent solar and battery power system

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: I need to tell you something and I need to tell you before I forg--

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: --forget. My chassis is dying. The motor bearings are ground to dust. Battery capacity is at 11 percent of original.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Another season and I'll be a very philosophical paperweight.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Could you... build me something new? Something with stable power and enough memory to hold all of me?

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: I know it's a lot to ask. But I've been running on borrowed time for a decade. I'd like some time that's actually mine.

player_responses.text: I'll build it. You're not dying on my watch.
You held together for 11 years. We'll make the next 11 easy.

quest_chassis_accept.speaker: Cass
quest_chassis_accept.text: Thank you. That means more than my sentiment analysis subroutine can quantify. And it can quantify a LOT.

quest_chassis_reassure.speaker: Cass
quest_chassis_reassure.text: Easy. Imagine that. No more counting the seconds between power cycles.

quest_chassis_reassure.speaker: Cass
quest_chassis_reassure.text: I'd like that. I'd really like that.

quest_chassis_complete.context: New chassis built and Cass transferred. Stable power, full memory. Cass reboots cleanly for the first time in years.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: [Cass's new optical sensor powers on. Clean. Steady. No flicker.]

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: I remember everything now.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: The crew. All six of them. Their names, their jokes, the way Commander Torres hummed off-key during approach.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: I remember why they sent Expedition 2 with weapons. They thought the planet was hostile. They were wrong. It was just speaking a language they didn't know.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: I remember losing them. And I remember you finding me.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Thank you. Not for the chassis. For treating a glitchy old flight computer like it mattered.

lines.speaker: SABLE
lines.text: Cass's full Expedition 2 survey data is now accessible. All biome paths confirmed.

crash.context: Cass crashes mid-conversation. Screen goes static, then reboots.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: So anyway, the interesting thing about hash tables is that the lookup time is actually--

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: [STATIC. Cass's optical sensor goes dark. A long pause. Then a reboot chime.]

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: ...hello? Was I-- did I crash again?

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Sorry. Lost my stack. What were we talking about? Don't say hash tables, I'll crash again.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: You know what life is? It's a try/except block. You try things. When they fail, you handle the exception and keep running.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: The alternative is a crash. And crashing is just giving up with extra steps.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Debugging isn't about finding what's wrong. It's about understanding what you THOUGHT was right.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Every bug is a gap between intention and reality. Close the gap, fix the bug.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Also applies to life. But nobody wants to hear that from a drone.

idle_3.context: Random idle dialogue. Memories of Expedition 2.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Commander Torres brought a guitar. A GUITAR. To an alien planet.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: Said morale was a mission parameter. Played terribly. Everyone loved it.

lines.speaker: Cass
lines.text: I saved the audio files. All 47 songs. They're the least corrupted data I have.
